coding-tips: Name booleans as questions: `isValid`, `hasAccess`, `canRetry`. Code reads like English.
Return early. Guard clauses at the top of a function beat deeply nested if/else pyramids.
Commit small and often. A commit should answer one question: what changed and why?
Don't comment WHAT the code does — comment WHY. The code already says what.
Use `.env` files for secrets and add them to `.gitignore` before your first commit, not after.
Learn your debugger. `console.log` / `print` debugging works, but breakpoints are 10x faster for tricky bugs.
Write the test first when fixing a bug — it proves the bug exists, then proves your fix works.
Delete dead code instead of commenting it out. Git remembers everything anyway.
Prefer composition over inheritance. Deep class hierarchies age badly.
Read error messages fully, from the top. The answer is usually in the first 3 lines.
Rate-limit and retry with exponential backoff whenever you call an external API.
Keep functions under ~30 lines. If you need a comment to separate 'sections', split the function.
Idempotency matters: running a script twice should not corrupt data or double-insert rows.
Validate input at the boundary (API/form), trust it internally. Don't re-validate everywhere.
Use meaningful commit messages: `fix: handle empty PDF pages in reader` beats `update code`.
Cache expensive calls, but always set a TTL. Stale cache bugs are the worst bugs.
SQL: always test your DELETE/UPDATE as a SELECT first.
Log at boundaries — incoming requests, outgoing calls, and failures. Not every line.
Handle the sad path first. Happy-path-only code is demo code, not production code.
Automate anything you've done manually three times.
languages: Python: list comprehensions are great until they need two lines of logic — then use a loop.
Python: `pathlib.Path` beats `os.path` string juggling for anything filesystem related.
JavaScript: `??` (nullish coalescing) only falls back on null/undefined, unlike `||` which also catches 0 and ''.
TypeScript: `unknown` is the safe `any`. It forces you to narrow the type before using it.
Python: `dict.get(key, default)` avoids KeyError and reads cleaner than try/except.
JavaScript: `Promise.allSettled` beats `Promise.all` when you want results even if some fail.
Go's error-as-value pattern feels verbose at first, then makes error handling impossible to forget.
Rust's borrow checker is basically a strict code reviewer that prevents whole classes of bugs at compile time.
SQL: `EXPLAIN` your slow queries before adding indexes blindly.
Python: f-strings support debugging syntax: `f'{value=}'` prints `value=42`.
Bash: always quote your variables — `"$file"` — or spaces in filenames will ruin your day.
JavaScript: `structuredClone()` does real deep copies; stop using `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify())`.
Python: `enumerate()` and `zip()` remove 90% of index-based loop bugs.
TypeScript: prefer union types (`'draft' | 'sent' | 'failed'`) over enums for simple states.
Regex: build it incrementally and test on real samples — regex101.com is your friend.
ai: Prompting: show the model 2-3 examples of the exact output format you want. Few-shot beats long instructions.
For structured output from LLMs, demand JSON-only responses and strip markdown fences before parsing.
RAG quality is mostly retrieval quality. A better chunking strategy beats a bigger model.
LLMs are confident when wrong. Always add a 'return null if unsure' escape hatch to extraction prompts.
Temperature 0 for extraction/classification tasks, higher only for creative generation.
Batch your LLM calls where possible — throughput and cost both improve dramatically.
Evaluate LLM pipelines with a small golden dataset before scaling. 50 hand-checked examples reveal a lot.
OCR + LLM beats OCR alone for scanned documents — the model fixes recognition errors using context.
Embeddings drift: if you change embedding models, re-embed everything. Mixed vector spaces give garbage results.
Prompt injection is real: never let retrieved/user content override your system instructions without sanitization.
Fine-tuning is rarely the answer; better prompts and retrieval usually get you there faster and cheaper.
Log every LLM input/output pair in production. You can't debug what you didn't capture.
Chain-of-thought helps reasoning tasks but bloats cost on simple ones — use it selectively.
Agentic loops need budgets: cap max steps, max tokens, and max tool calls or they'll run forever.
trending-projects: **Ollama** — run open-source LLMs locally with one command. Great for offline prototyping. https://github.com/ollama/ollama
**shadcn/ui** — copy-paste React components you actually own, built on Radix + Tailwind. https://github.com/shadcn-ui/ui
**uv** — Rust-based Python package manager, 10-100x faster than pip. https://github.com/astral-sh/uv
**Excalidraw** — hand-drawn style whiteboard, perfect for architecture diagrams. https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw
**LangGraph** — build stateful, multi-step LLM agents as graphs. https://github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph
**Zod** — TypeScript-first schema validation; parse, don't validate. https://github.com/colinhacks/zod
**DuckDB** — SQLite for analytics; query CSVs and Parquet files with SQL, no server. https://github.com/duckdb/duckdb
**FastAPI** — Python APIs with automatic docs and validation from type hints. https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi
**Bruno** — open-source, git-friendly API client (Postman alternative). https://github.com/usebruno/bruno
**Marker** — converts PDFs to markdown with high accuracy, handles scanned docs. https://github.com/VikParuchuri/marker
**LiteLLM** — one interface for 100+ LLM APIs, with fallbacks and cost tracking. https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm
**Playwright** — reliable browser automation for testing and scraping. https://github.com/microsoft/playwright
articles: "Choose Boring Technology" by Dan McKinley — why proven tools beat shiny ones. https://mcfunley.com/choose-boring-technology
"The Grug Brained Developer" — a humble guide to avoiding complexity. https://grugbrain.dev
"How to Do Great Work" by Paul Graham. http://paulgraham.com/greatwork.html
"Parse, Don't Validate" by Alexis King — a mindset shift for handling data. https://lexi-lambda.github.io/blog/2019/11/05/parse-don-t-validate/
"The Twelve-Factor App" — timeless principles for building deployable services. https://12factor.net
"Things You Should Never Do" by Joel Spolsky — why rewrites from scratch usually fail. https://www.joelonsoftware.com/2000/04/06/things-you-should-never-do-part-i/
"What Every Programmer Should Know About Memory" (skim the intro at least). https://people.freebsd.org/~lstewart/articles/cpumemory.pdf
"Falsehoods Programmers Believe About Names" — humbling and useful. https://www.kalzumeus.com/2010/06/17/falsehoods-programmers-believe-about-names/
"You Are Not Google" — pick tech for your scale, not theirs. https://blog.bradfieldcs.com/you-are-not-google-84912cf44afb
"Write code that is easy to delete, not easy to extend." https://programmingisterrible.com/post/139222674273/write-code-that-is-easy-to-delete-not-easy-to
